default: Price the active pressure line before choosing status, Explosion, or a pivot.
If direct damage, Roar/Whirlwind, or staying in already improves a named route,
do not switch to a status script just because the move exists. If the next hit
changes a support piece's future job, keep attacking until the opponent shows
the branch that flips the exchange.
Item-removal extension: when a status-capable lead or special attacker faces an
obvious bulky receiver, rank Thief or equivalent item removal beside sleep and
coverage before choosing status. Item removal is top only if it changes a named
route: recovery denial, hazard clock, sleep-turn pressure, damage threshold, or
reset-hub removal. It also requires public no-item evidence for the user, or an
explicit high-risk read with a fallback; full HP with no Leftovers message is
not by itself no-item evidence.
Seed/item-pressure extension: when Leech Seed is revealed or strongly implied
and the item map shows a no-item user into a Leftovers receiver, re-rank seed
clock, Thief, Explosion, and switch-reset before sleep script. This is strongest
after public clues such as no Leftovers recovery, a seeded bulky target, or a
receiver that loses a route when its item is stolen.
Support-lead order extension: Smeargle-like utility leads are not sleep-only.
After Spikes, Thunder Wave, Baton Pass, Agility, or another support move is
revealed or strongly implied, re-rank active damage, field ordering, and
receiver handoffs before switching to a generic sleep absorber. If the support
lead is frail and the active hit or field move converts now, pressure can beat
the absorber. If Agility is public before Spore, treat Baton Pass and the
receiver map as live; the threat of sleep may have already done its job by
forcing the defender's switch.
Rest-capable pressure extension: Toxic or another timer does not automatically
force a bulky attacker out when Rest, Sleep Talk, or a known recovery route is
live. If the next hit changes a support piece's HP threshold, Explosion value,
Spin/Spikes job, or receiver map before Rest clears the clock, rank that active
pressure before a generic preservation switch.
exceptions: If direct pressure only creates damage without a route, status may be the
real converter.
If the opponent has a clean absorber or counter-pivot, status may hand them
the board.
If the opponent's best branch is a switch you can name, compare one more hit
to the counter-switch instead of attacking by inertia.
If the support piece has no future entry or job, spending it may be correct.
policy_cards.title: Active Pressure Before Status
trigger: A status-capable Pokemon can use Sleep Powder, Hypnosis, Toxic, Thunder Wave,
or a similar move.
A support piece can cash out with Explosion or switch to a generic answer.
The active attack, phaze, or pressure line already threatens damage, switches,
hazard conversion, or role preservation.
A bulky attacker is repeatedly forcing the opponent into resist or support
answers.
policy_cards.use_when: a Pokemon with status, Explosion, or a pivot option also has a direct
policy_cards.worst_branch: You see a status or Explosion button, press it automatically, and give up an active route that was already forcing damage, switches, hazards, or recovery.

default: After naming the branch, name the action that beats that branch. Choose
counter-switch, coverage, phaze, Reflect, Toxic, Screech, setup, status,
Explosion, or sacrifice only if that action beats the named receiver or punish.
Substitute/setup extension: if the expected branch is an immunity or hard
receiver, rank Substitute or setup beside coverage and counter-handoff. It can
be the positive-selection action when it keeps the active route while exposing
or punishing the receiver.
Utility-screen/drop extension: if Reflect, Light Screen, Screech, Dynamic
Punch, Toxic, or a similar utility move changes the named receiver map, rank it
as a branch-punish candidate before default damage or switch. The support move
is top only when it creates a concrete follow-up: phaze, Pursuit, Rock Slide,
Explosion, Spin after Ghost coverage, or a safer handoff. If the utility move is
only possible-only, keep the fallback line explicit.
Revealed-package extension: when a move reveal shows a coherent package, re-solve
the whole route before continuing the old plan. Substitute into a RestTalk
target may be passive scouting by itself, but if Nightmare, confusion, Leech
Seed, Growth, Baton Pass, or phazing support is revealed or strongly implied,
rank the package follow-up above generic damage unless a revealed reset, phaze,
faster breaker, or low-cost switch invalidates it.
Revealed-coverage blocker extension: when a support piece reveals coverage that
hits the named blocker or spinblocker, re-rank that coverage before a safe
handoff. The support piece may have become the converter for this board.
Revealed coverage over STAB extension: once Snorlax, Steelix, or another
attacker reveals coverage that hits the named Ghost, Rock, Steel, or spinner
branch, rank that revealed coverage above generic STAB into the immunity or
resist. Do not demote the revealed answer just because the species's default
damage button is stronger into the active target.
Pursuit-punish extension: when a Ghost or Psychic route piece has just blocked
Spin or forced progress, name the Pursuit user that can enter. If coverage,
Dynamic Punch, status, Explosion, or a lower-value switch beats that Pursuit
branch, rank it before a generic retreat.
Pass/Substitute package extension: when Baton Pass, Growth, Substitute,
coverage, sleep, or phaze appears in the same route, name the package before
choosing. Dry Baton Pass to a status threat, Substitute after absorbing the
faster hit, coverage into a low phazer, or a counter-handoff to the receiver can
all beat a generic safe switch if they win the next board.
Growth/recovery package extension: when Espeon, Vaporeon, Jolteon, or another
booster shows Growth, Morning Sun, Baton Pass, or coverage in the same route,
rank the package-denial action before generic Pursuit, chip, or retreat. The
top action must either remove the booster, phaze it, status the package, hit
the revealed coverage branch, or hand off to the owner that survives the next
boosted turn.
Dry-pass reveal extension: if a fast Electric or support piece has shown dry
Baton Pass as a handoff, its next appearance must reopen Agility, Growth,
Substitute, receiver, and receiver-denial lines. Do not collapse Baton Pass into
a generic switch method once the package is public.
Compound Baton Pass extension: when Agility, Acid Armor, Growth, Swords Dance,
Belly Drum, Roar, or Substitute appears with Baton Pass, keep an explicit boost
and receiver ledger until the boosts are phazed, the receiver faints, or the
chain is otherwise reset. Choose the action that beats the receiver board:
phaze, screen, paralysis, damage to put the receiver in revenge range, or a
counter-handoff. Do not continue a sleep/status script after the pass route is
public unless that status hits the receiver or enables the phaze/revenge turn.
Passed-Fighting receiver extension: when Agility-pass or speed support reaches
Machamp or another Fighting breaker, price Cross Chop, Rock Slide, coverage,
setup, and the high-crit branch before deciding Snorlax can stay. A Ghost
handoff is not automatic if it gives the receiver a free setup or coverage
turn; active damage is correct when it puts the receiver into a named revenge
or phaze range. Staying is wrong when the Fighting hit can remove the route
piece before that threshold is achieved.
Revealed-lure coverage extension: when a Pokemon reveals off-standard coverage
into its normal answer, immediately re-rank future turns around that reveal.
Before the reveal, keep the lure in strong-prior or possible-only tiers; after
the reveal, do not demote it because the species's default role points
elsewhere.
Revealed-lure second-turn extension: after a lure move such as Thunder, Fire
Blast, Hidden Power, or coverage into a support answer becomes public, the next
turn is often about the absorber that blanks the lure. Name that absorber class
and the counter-handoff owner before repeating the active lure. Keep the active
move top only if it removes the target now or the absorber branch is weaker.
Counter-handoff extension: when the current active is likely to leave, name the
next board and our owner for that board. If that owner enters safely and
creates immediate progress, the top action is usually the handoff rather than
damage into the Pokemon that is about to leave.
Early-cycle handoff extension: in opening GSC loops after Spikes, Toxic, Rest,
or an obvious forced switch, identify whether the next board belongs to
Snorlax, Forretress/Cloyster, an Electric, a Ghost, Skarmory, or a phazer. The
top action must either move that owner in, hit that owner, or explain why the
active pressure still converts through the expected handoff.
Repeated-cycle extension: once the same defensive cycle repeats twice, treat
the next node as a revealed or strong-prior branch until public evidence breaks
it. Choose the move or handoff that wins that node, not the move that merely
would have been safe on the previous node. In spectator-public work, score a
hidden exact teammate by class, but keep a fallback to a revealed owner.
Ground-breaker receiver triangle extension: when Marowak, Rhydon, Steelix, or
a similar Ground/physical breaker enters on a paralyzed Electric or forced
Normal-resist loop, name the receiver before clicking STAB. Skarmory and
Forretress ask for Fire coverage or a handoff if Fire is only possible-only;
Starmie, Suicune, Vaporeon, and other Waters ask for Earthquake, setup, or a
preservation switch; Ghosts ask for Earthquake, Rock Slide, status, or a
Pursuit/handoff. Do not let Earthquake stay top into a named Skarmory branch.
Status-absorber extension: when the named branch is a sleep or status absorber,
do not keep the status move on top by habit. Re-rank cash-out, coverage,
counter-switch, setup, or Substitute into that absorber before choosing the
original status move.
Status-into-switch extension: if the visible active is already poisoned,
immune, or likely to leave, status can still be the branch-punish when the
named switch target can be statused and that status changes the route more
than coverage into the current active. Choose the status only after naming the
incoming target and fallback if it stays.
Status-tolerant receiver extension: when the opposing support move is Toxic,
Thunder Wave, or sleep, include status-tolerant receivers such as Snorlax,
Ground-types, already statused pieces, or RestTalk users alongside type
immunities. The correct handoff is the one that preserves or creates the next
converter, not always the one that blocks Electric damage.
Setup-into-receiver extension: when an active physical converter forces a
Starmie, Zapdos, Snorlax, Ghost, or phazer receiver, rank Curse/setup beside
coverage and immediate damage. Setup is the branch-punish only if it changes
the receiver board before the receiver can recover, status, phaze, or KO.
CurseLax coverage-receiver extension: after Snorlax reveals Curse, solve the
receiver map before choosing another setup turn. Compare coverage into the
named Ghost, Cloyster, Skarmory/phazer, Rock/Steel, or sleeping RestTalk branch
against another Curse, Rest, and handoff. Revealed or side-known coverage can
anchor the top line; strong-prior coverage must name the fallback if absent;
possible-only coverage stays a branch unless the answer is explicitly a
high-risk read.
Boosted-timer conversion extension: after a Snorlax-like converter has already
banked boosts and poison, Toxic, recoil, Perish, or phaze pressure starts the
clock, re-rank attack, Rest, more setup, and handoff by the next two turns.
Strong-prior Rest is not automatically top. If revealed or standard STAB now
chunks the receiver, forces a Rest/phaze/explosion decision, or preserves the
boosted route before the timer steals it, rank the cash-out attack before
generic preservation.
CurseLax Rest-clock extension: when CurseLax faces revealed RestTalk Electric
or special pressure, compare Double-Edge, Rest, another Curse, and handoff
against the next Thunder/Sleep Talk/wake turn. Curse is top only if the extra
boost changes the post-Rest or receiver board; Rest is top when the next hit
would remove the boosted route; attack is top when it forces the irreversible
trade before the clock.
Sleep-clause absorber extension: when the opponent already has a sleeping
RestTalk or otherwise useful sleeping piece, price the switch that brings it
back in to blank further sleep attempts and preserve sleep-clause value. Choose
status only if it still improves through that absorber.
RestTalk receiver re-entry extension: after Rest or Sleep Talk is public, a
sleeping Electric, Snorlax, bulky Water, or other RestTalk user can be the
named receiver even while asleep. Price the switch, Sleep Talk roll, and wake
clock as revealed route facts, not as possible-only hidden-team anchors.
Cash-out immunity guard: before Explosion, self-sacrifice, or an all-in trade,
name revealed immunities and the legal hidden immunity class that would blank
the trade in no-Team-Preview play. If the trade spends an irreplaceable piece
and loses to a plausible immunity branch, prefer pressure, setup, phaze,
coverage, or a handoff unless the line is explicitly marked as a high-risk
read.
Handoff boundary: before switching, compare active pressure, coverage/status/
phaze/setup into the expected branch, and the handoff. If the current move
already affects the next board after switch cost, Spikes damage, recoil, and
information loss are priced, it can beat the handoff.
exceptions: Spectator-public hidden bench may make the exact counter-switch unknowable;
score by class when the route action is correct.
Setup is correct when the expected receiver cannot reset the route or when
the boost changes the next board more than meeting the receiver now.
If a set reveal changes the role, re-solve instead of importing the standard
species job.
If the active move already improves through the expected receiver, do not
hand off just to look predictive.
If the package spends the user's own HP or tempo and the opponent can break
it without losing route equity, treat the package as self-tax rather than
automatic progress.
policy_cards.title: Branch Action After Naming
trigger: You can name a likely receiver, absorber, hard answer, or support branch.
The tempting move only improves the visible active matchup.
A revealed move or set detail changes which action beats the branch.
The active KO or active trade looks obvious, but the opponent can deny it by
moving a receiver or sack into the line.
policy_cards.use_when: the opponent is likely to switch, reveal coverage, use a support
policy_cards.worst_branch: You correctly say "they switch to the answer," then choose a move that only beats the Pokemon currently on screen and hand the opponent the board.

default: Name the support job before spending the piece. Preserve if the job is still
route-defining and no immediate converter appears. If you explicitly name an
Explosion or Self-Destruct branch, the recommendation must also name the
absorber, sack, immunity, or reason staying still preserves the route.
Defending mirror: before attacking a low self-KO candidate with a valuable
active, name speed/order, whether the active survives with its route job, and
the lower-value owner that absorbs or resists the trade. If the self-KO moves
first or the active does not survive usefully, prefer the owner unless active
pressure or coverage wins a concrete route immediately.
Route-defining gate: self-KO leads only when the prior support job is delivered
or irrelevant, the target and post-trade converter are named, delay lets that
target reset or escape, and no revealed or cheap absorber covers the trade. If
support is not delivered yet, the target is not exact, or active pressure /
coverage improves the branch without spending the piece, treat self-KO as a
branch rather than the main line.
Undelivered-support override: a support job can become irrelevant when the
piece is low, will die before converting the field state, and has drawn the
exact blocker that opens the next owner. In that case, coverage plus Explosion
or another cash-out can beat laying the first Spikes layer. Still name the
absorber, immunity, lost support job, and post-trade owner before making the
cash-out top.
Named-absorber cash-out: when a one-time piece has drawn the exact absorber or
route blocker it is meant to remove, Explosion or sacrifice can be the
positive branch-punish even if the user is not low. Require the target, lost
role, revealed resist/Ghost/Protect/Substitute branches, possible-only hidden
immunity class, and post-trade route owner before making the trade top.
Low-Cloyster four-way check: after Cloyster has delivered Spikes, Toxic, or
coverage pressure and is low enough to be forced soon, solve preservation,
coverage/status, Explosion, and defensive sack together. Before Explosion,
name revealed Ghosts and plausible hidden Ghost or low-value guards; before
preserving, name the pressure owner that enters and what Cloyster still does
later.
Named-resist check: if the likely absorber is a revealed Normal resist or a
high-defense owner such as Rhydon, Steelix, Golem, or boosted Cloyster, do not
stop at naming Explosion. Rank coverage, setup, drop, or phaze that beats that
absorber before the self-KO. The cash-out is top only after the resist branch is
priced and still fails to preserve the opponent's route.
Defensive sack owner: spending a support piece does not always mean pressing
Explosion. If a low or doomed support piece can absorb the expected attack and
preserve a higher-value route owner, the switch-sack can be the positive move.
Name the preserved piece and next board before choosing it.
Full-health active-pressure boundary: do not promote Explosion or Self-Destruct
from possible to primary just because the species can cash out. If the user is
healthy and still has coverage, status, or pivot pressure that improves the
route, price that active pressure before spending it.
Forretress defensive-Explosion gate: Forretress can stop or slow boosted
threats with Explosion, but its main value is often Spikes, Spin, Toxic,
coverage, and Normal-resist compression. Before making unrevealed Explosion
top, name the boosted threat, whether Forretress must trade now, what damage
or free turn the trade creates through Defense boosts, who receives the next
board, and what the fallback is if Forretress only has coverage or support.
Defending support-Explosion guard: when the opposing support piece has already
delivered Spikes, Spin, status, or a pass handoff and can now trade with
Explosion, name revealed Ghosts, Protect/Substitute shields, Rock/Steel
resists, and low-value sacks before attacking. If the immunity owner can enter
without losing the route, rank that guard before active damage into the boom
user.
Low-HP Rest race: before spending a low route piece with recoil, Explosion,
Self-Destruct, or a likely last attack into a low bulky target, price whether
the target can Rest before the hit. If the faster target can heal first, the
attack may become a self-KO or bad cash-out into a preserved route piece.
exceptions: If the piece has no realistic entry path, its future job may be imaginary.
If the opponent's worst branch turns the preserved piece into a free setup or
Spin window, preservation may be worse than spending.
If the current position is losing slowly, accept a concrete high-variance
converter over passive preservation.
policy_cards.title: Cash-Out Boundary
trigger: A support piece is low, statused, or hard to re-enter.
The active move can open a concrete converter, not just create damage.
Preserving the piece may retain Spikes, phazing, sleep absorption, pivoting,
boom pressure, or a needed check.
policy_cards.use_when: a low or one-time support piece can either preserve a future job or
policy_cards.worst_branch: You preserve a famous or sentimental piece, lose the current converter window, and later discover the preserved piece had no safe entry or no remaining job. The opposite severe branch is naming the boom, leaving the route piece in by habit, and losing the route on the forced revenge turn.

default: Treat Spikes as a route subgame. Ask whether the line sets, retains, removes,
punishes Spin, or converts the entry map. Hazard progress is not real if the
opponent can remove it without giving up a route-relevant cost.
Hazard job ordering: before choosing Rapid Spin, ask whether the hazard piece
can set missing Spikes on the opponent's side right now. If one side is
unspiked and the setter is already active, setting the missing Spikes may beat
clearing owned-side Spikes.
Tempo coverage before layer: if the support piece can immediately remove,
slow, or force out the active threat while surviving, coverage such as Surf,
Icy Wind, Hidden Power, or Toxic can beat the first Spikes layer. The layer is
top when it converts before the support piece is forced out or killed.
Cloyster mirror branch extension: before Cloyster clicks Rapid Spin into
Skarmory, opposing Cloyster, or another passive hazard-cycle piece, name the
opponent's next owner. If opposing Cloyster enters, Surf, Toxic, Icy Wind, or
Explosion can convert more than Spin because they damage the setter/spinner
that keeps the loop alive. If the owner is a flyer, Electric, Ghost, or phazer,
rank the counter-handoff, coverage, status, or Spin by which action beats that
owner, not by the field job alone.
Spin side-ownership gate: before recommending Rapid Spin, explicitly name
whose side has Spikes. If the spinner's own side is clean, Spin is not hazard
progress and should fall below pressure, status, Recover, coverage, or handoff
unless the move's chip effect itself matters.
Setup-threat boundary: if a boosted or immediately boosting Pokemon is already
converting, status, phazing, or cash-out may outrank both setting missing
Spikes and clearing owned-side Spikes.
Revealed phaze-loop commitment: once Whirlwind or Roar is public and Spikes is
already converting, keep the phaze loop ranked until the opponent shows a
public branch that removes hazards, forces the phazer out, wins a Rest race, or
opens a better converter. Do not drop the loop for generic chip or a safe switch
without naming what breaks the loop.
Post-Spin reset check: after a successful Rapid Spin, immediately ask whether
the opposing setter is active, alive, and free to reset Spikes. Clearing hazards
is not stable progress if the setter can restore them before paying a real
route cost.
Post-Spin Recover check: after Starmie or another recovering spinner clears
Spikes and survives the shown hit, ask what stops the Recover loop. Repeating
weak damage is not conversion if the spinner can heal through it; rank Electric
pressure, Toxic/status, Pursuit/trapping, Explosion/cash-out, or a double-switch
to the support receiver before repeating the same attack.
SubSpin shield extension: when Starmie or another spinner reveals Substitute
before Rapid Spin, treat Substitute as a status shield and spin enabler, not
just HP loss. Against a faster SubSpin Starmie, Toxic, sleep, and Explosion may
hit the barrier instead of the real spinner, and an immediate Spikes reset can
hand over a protected Spin cycle. Rank pressure handoff, breaking the
Substitute, or named spinner cash-out before repeating support.
SubStarmie pressure extension: Substitute Starmie may be a Surf pressure route
even when Spin is not immediate. If it is behind a Substitute against Thunder,
price Thunder accuracy, shield break, Surf chip, Recover, and Rest-forcing
before assuming the Electric answer already owns the board.
Spinblock coverage extension: when Gengar or another spinblocker enters the
hazard loop and the setter has revealed coverage that damages it meaningfully,
price active coverage before pivoting or trying Spin. The spinblocker is not
stable if the current board can force it low or out.
Rock spinner package extension: Golem can be a Normal-resistant phazer,
Explosion threat, and Rapid Spin user at the same time. When it enters after
hazards are up, price Spin, Roar, Explosion, and Earthquake together before
calling it only a Ground or boom branch.
Tentacruel spinner package extension: Tentacruel can reveal Rapid Spin without
being a generic setup Water. After Spin is public, rank Surf, Protect scouting,
switch, and Spin as one package. Protect can buy Leftovers and reveal the
spinblock response before the next Spin or handoff.
Spinblock-to-Pursuit chain: after a Ghost blocks Spin, immediately ask whether
Tyranitar, Umbreon, or another Pursuit user can enter. If the spinblocker has
revealed coverage or Dynamic Punch into that Pursuit user, rank the punish; if
not, preserve or sack with the Pursuit tax explicit.
Forretress compression extension: Forretress is primarily a Spikes piece, but
it also compresses Normal resistance, Rapid Spin, and defensive Explosion.
When it enters a Snorlax or Zapdos route, compare first-layer Spikes against
the special handoff, the Normal-resist job, and the emergency trade. Do not
replace Forretress with a generic Ghost or Skarmory branch without naming what
Forretress uniquely converts.
Forretress move-tier extension: after Forretress reveals some of Spikes, Toxic,
Rapid Spin, Hidden Power, Giga Drain, Rest, or Explosion, keep the remaining
slots tiered as revealed, strong-prior, or possible-only. Explosion is common
and can be the emergency route-denial move, but Hidden Power/coverage chip,
Spin, Spikes, Toxic into a switch, or a handoff may be the converting action.
Do not make an unrevealed Explosion the main line unless the boosted threat,
target, post-trade owner, and fallback if Explosion is absent are explicit.
Steelix phaze/setup split: with Spikes down and Roar revealed, Steelix can
convert by repeatedly phazing expected counters. Keep Roar top while the
switch loop is stable. If the opponent's loop-breaker is a spinner, Skarmory,
Forretress, or another piece that lets Steelix set up or forces a different
resource trade, rank Curse, coverage, or handoff before autopiloting Roar.
Curse can be correct before Roar when Steelix has just forced Raikou,
Tyranitar, or another target that is likely to leave and the receiver cannot
immediately remove Steelix. After the receiver reveals coverage that hits
Steelix, price the damage clock before choosing another boost; Roar or handoff
beats Curse if the coverage will stop the phaze from resolving.
exceptions: A spinner without an entry path is not an immediate Spin threat.
A spinblocker without a safe entry is not real coverage.
If the spinner is already status-controlled and the setter still has a
future entry/job, hand off to pressure before resetting or spending by habit.
If the setter is already active after Spin, resetting Spikes may be the best
immediate job even while status-controlled.
If the setter is already active before Spin and the opponent's side is
unspiked, setting Spikes may be the best immediate job before any clear.
If a setup threat is already live, stop the route first; hazard work is only
top if the threat cannot convert through it.
If a support piece has just done its job, identify the counter-pivot before
assuming the hazard loop is stable.
policy_cards.title: Hazard Loop Spin Window
trigger: Spikes can be set, retained, removed, or converted.
Rapid Spin or a spinner entry path is live.
The current move looks like generic progress but may create a Spin window.
policy_cards.use_when: Spikes, Rapid Spin, spinblocking, or post-support handoff determines
policy_cards.worst_branch: You call setting Spikes progress, give the opponent a free spinner sequence, and lose both hazard pressure and tempo.

default: Treat the voluntary entry or set reveal as evidence. Price lure moves,
coverage, cash-out jobs, and one-turn support actions before assigning the
standard role. Use "possible" language until the move is public.
Use a three-tier public-information gate:
`revealed`: public and usable as fact.
`strong prior`: affects risk pricing, but the line must still name the
fallback if wrong.
`possible only`: branch to cover or mention, not the reason for the main move
unless the answer is explicitly a high-risk read.
Item-state gate: moves such as Thief, RestTalk item denial, or no-item lure
logic need public item-state evidence before becoming the main line. A Pokemon
at full HP that does not show Leftovers has not revealed no item. If item state
is only possible, rank the move as a read and give the fallback.
exceptions: Spectator-public mode may not reveal the team context that made the entry
safe; score by decision class when exact role is unknowable.
A low or trapped piece may enter as a sack, not as a lure.
A standard role can still be correct after an off-standard reveal if the next
board is owned by that standard job.
policy_cards.title: Hidden Role Voluntary Entry
trigger: A piece enters a matchup where the standard role would look passive or bad.
A revealed move changes the role more than the species name does.
The tempting line assumes a common move, item, or role that has not been
revealed.
policy_cards.use_when: a Pokemon voluntarily enters a bad-looking matchup, reveals an
policy_cards.worst_branch: You import a common set from species memory, miss the lure or one-time job, and choose a support move that lets the opponent's actual route convert. Opposite worst branch: You overcorrect from prior hidden-role misses, promote a possible-only move to fact, and choose a line that fails if the unshown move is not real.

default: Treat external GSC knowledge as source material, not local truth. If the local
status is unknown or supplied-but-unverified, cap confidence and say what
fixture, source check, debugger output, or emulator trace would settle it.
exceptions: Source-visible boss rosters and known boss opener policy are allowed for
player-side Gym Leader Lab advice.
Haki/oracle behavior stays quarantined and does not generalize to ordinary
boss AI.
Ordinary boss AI must not use unrevealed player team slots, hidden moves,
hidden items, hidden PP, exact hidden stats, or current-turn player input.
policy_cards.title: Romhack Mechanics Firewall
trigger: A recommendation depends on mechanics that may differ from vanilla GSC.
The claim touches Spikes/Rapid Spin, sleep, RestTalk, Explosion, phazing,
type passives, items, AI information, Counter/Mirror Coat, or move timing.
A source is expert vanilla GSC material rather than local evidence.
policy_cards.use_when: external GSC knowledge affects a local romhack move, route, damage,
policy_cards.worst_branch: You import a vanilla mechanic into a local boss route, choose a move that only works in vanilla, and then count the failure as a planning error instead of a mechanics error.

default: Treat sleep as a route-state change, not a script. A Pokemon put to sleep is
often switched out and preserved to exploit Sleep Clause value instead of
burning wake turns and becoming sleep bait again. Before staying in, name what
the sleeper gains by burning turns now.
Track sleep by side. If the opponent has not slept one of our Pokemon yet, a
live Hypnosis, Lovely Kiss, Sleep Powder, or similar move still demands an
absorber plan even if we already slept one of theirs.
Sleeping status-absorber handoff: a Rested or otherwise sleeping RestTalk
piece can switch into Toxic, Thunder Wave, or a sleep move to blank the status
while preserving the active route piece. Before doing this, name the sleeping
piece's next job, wake/Sleep Talk value, and the opponent's counter-pivot.
Lead sleep handoff extension: after a lead sleep absorber takes sleep, ask
whether its job is already complete. If Sleep Talk is not revealed, compare
preserving the sleeper and moving the next-board owner against attacking or
burning sleep. Possible-only coverage into the sleeping absorber cannot outrank
the public handoff unless it is explicitly a high-risk read with fallback.
RestTalk route-piece extension: in GSC, a sleeping RestTalk user can still make
progress and can even reset to full if Sleep Talk calls Rest. After Rest or
Sleep Talk is revealed, treat that sleeper as a possible active receiver for
Electric, special, status, or bulky-water boards when its class fits the branch.
Rest wake-count extension: after Rest in GSC, count two sleeping action turns,
then expect the Pokemon to wake and act on the next turn. If a helper prompt
still displays `slp`, manual Rest timing wins; do not recommend Sleep Talk on
the wake-and-act turn. If the Rest user switches out before a `cant|slp` or
Sleep Talk turn, those sleep turns have not been spent; resume the count when
it re-enters.
Rest full-HP failure extension: Rest fails at full HP. If a paralyzed,
poisoned, burned, or frozen Pokemon tries Rest while still at full HP, do not
mark the status cured or start a sleep counter unless the log shows Rest
succeeded.
Revealed-RestTalk boundary: before Sleep Talk is public, treat RestTalk as a
strong prior only when the species and board support it, and keep a fallback.
After Sleep Talk is public, a sleeping Electric, Zapdos, Snorlax, or similar
piece can stay active and convert with Sleep Talk instead of switching out by
default.
RestTalk pressure into setup extension: when a revealed RestTalk sleeper faces
a boosting Snorlax, Electric, Water, or similar converter, do not treat the
sleeper as passive. Its next Sleep Talk or wake action can force attack, Rest,
phaze, or handoff timing before the booster gets another free turn.
Cleric reset check: after Heal Bell, re-solve the whole status map. Formerly
poisoned or paralyzed absorbers may now be vulnerable to sleep/status, and a
sleeping cleric may wake and reset the team again.
exceptions: Do not assume the revealed sleeper is the future sleep source or absorber.
Do not assign Lovely Kiss, RestTalk, or coverage from species alone.
RestTalk possibility is not proof to stay; if the opponent's counter-pivot
takes over, switch out and save the sleeper.
Do not overcorrect: revealed RestTalk can stay when no public teammate owns
the branch better, but wake-count tracking must be exact.
If the opponent's best punish is a double, setup, Explosion, or free Spikes,
preserving the sleeper may still lose tempo.
If the opponent acts before a Rest user wakes, status into the still-sleeping
target may fail; price damage, hazards, setup, or handoff instead.
If a low target can Rest before our attack lands, do not treat current HP as
the target. Re-score against the healed or sleeping board before choosing
recoil, self-KO, or last-hit damage.
policy_cards.title: Sleep Absorber And Set Ambiguity
trigger: A Pokemon has been put to sleep.
The sleeper's future value depends on Sleep Clause, wake turns, RestTalk, or
being saved as an absorber.
Snorlax or another bulky pivot has unrevealed set information that changes
the correct response.
policy_cards.use_when: sleep, RestTalk, Lovely Kiss, sleep absorber choice, or Snorlax set
policy_cards.worst_branch: You burn sleep turns out of habit, the sleeper wakes into no job, then gets put back to sleep or gives the opponent a free converter.

default: After support lands, immediately name the next board. Identify the converter,
the opponent's best counter-pivot, and the resource that must be preserved for
the handoff.
Screen/drop sequence extension: Reflect, Light Screen, Screech, and Dynamic
Punch are often the first half of the route, not the completion. After the
screen, Defense drop, or confusion lands, immediately rank the physical
follow-up, Pursuit/phaze, spinblock/Spin branch, or handoff that the support
move enabled.
Damage-threshold follow-up extension: Curse, Reflect, Light Screen, paralysis,
and similar support may make the next route-converting action direct damage
rather than preservation. Before switching out after support succeeds, ask
whether the enabled attack puts the receiver into revenge, phaze, Explosion, or
hazard range. If it does, damage can be the handoff.
Status-sacrifice extension: a low support piece can spend itself on Thunder
Wave, Toxic, screen, or similar utility if that action names and enables the
next converter. Do not call the sacrifice positive because status landed; call
it positive only when the receiver, the follow-up Pokemon, and the next board
are explicit.
Seed/reset handoff extension: Leech Seed, Thief, Rest, and phazing are not
complete just because they happened. After one lands, name whether the next
board is seed reset, item-denial pressure, RestTalk receiver re-entry, phaze
loop, or immediate cash-out, then rank the move that preserves or converts that
specific board.
Rest-to-counter-pivot extension: after a wall uses Rest, do not assume the
sleeping wall is the next target. First ask whether it stays to burn sleep
turns, has Sleep Talk, or leaves to a counter-pivot before spending any sleep
turn. If it can switch out and a revealed coverage move hits that pivot, rank
the pivot punish before generic damage into the Rest user.
Scout-to-conversion extension: Protect, scouting, and status cures are only
useful if they change the next action. After scouting a switch or revealing
coverage, immediately rank the active follow-up before defaulting to a handoff.
exceptions: If the support move revealed new set information, re-solve before following
the old handoff.
If the converter has no entry path, the handoff is not live.
If the opponent's worst branch is immediate setup, recovery, Spin, or
Explosion, cover that branch before polishing the route.
If the support piece is likely to be removed this turn, take the unique
support action first when it changes the route.
policy_cards.title: Support Handoff After Job
trigger: A support action succeeded.
The original plan assumed that success was enough.
The opponent has a counter-pivot, Spin, Rest, Explosion, setup, phaze, or
double-switch branch that can erase the progress.
policy_cards.use_when: a support move, phaze, status, hazard, scout, or lure has just landed
policy_cards.worst_branch: You celebrate the support action, miss the counter-pivot, and let the opponent erase the route before the converter enters.
